name: Eastern-Style Focaccia
intro: I love focaccia. Its pillowy texture and olive oil-soaked, salted crust give life to the idea of the humble loaf. When I master a baking recipe, I tend to experiment with adding ingredients and come up with a variety of versions. This Eastern-style focaccia was one of the more successful trials and is great with salty cheeses or for livening up a humble bowl of soup. It also makes for fabulous sandwiches (add feta and roasted peppers, for instance) and provides a superb accompaniment to dips such as hummus and the like.

ingredients.item: sea salt, crushed, plus extra for topping

method: Mix the soured cream with the cold and boiling water in a large bowl. Add the salt with the sugar and yeast, then blend in the flour, 2 tablespoons of the cumin seeds, the ground coriander, dried mint and the chilli flakes (if using) until the mixture forms a rough ball. Cover the bowl with clingfilm and leave the dough to rest somewhere warm for 10 minutes to rise.

Line a large roasting tin with some nonstick baking paper and place the dough on top. Stretch out the dough to the size of your tin, then using your fingers, poke deep holes all over it. Try not to pierce the dough, but you can be pretty tough with it. Cover the tin with clingfilm and leave the dough to rest somewhere warm for 1 hour (or longer, if you like).

Once the hour is up, preheat the oven to 200°C/fan 180°C/gas mark 6. Remove the clingfilm covering from the dough and drizzle the extra virgin olive oil over it, ensuring it covers every nook and cranny of the dough. A silicone brush will aid this process. Sprinkle the entire surface of the dough liberally with uncrushed sea salt flakes, thyme leaves, nigella seeds, the remaining cumin seeds and the sumac. Place the baking sheet on the top shelf of the oven and bake for 25–30 minutes, or until golden brown.
